title: Listening - Part 4: Running a Refill Shop
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Oliver, the owner of a refill shop. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest today is Oliver Kent, who runs a shop where customers refill containers instead of buying new packaging. Oliver, where did that idea come from? || Oliver: I used to organise events for a hotel. At the end of each conference we cleared away hundreds of partly used bottles and tiny packets. [24]Seeing so much packaging thrown out after only a few hours made me feel I should try a different kind of retail business[/24]. I began researching refill shops while I was still working there. || Presenter: What did you expect when you opened? || Oliver: I thought customers would walk in already knowing exactly how refilling worked. Some did, but many stood near the door looking worried about making a mistake. [25]I soon realised that simple instructions and friendly help were more necessary than offering a huge range of products[/25]. We put weighing steps on signs and keep spare jars for first-time visitors. || Presenter: What do people generally buy first? || Oliver: I expected fashionable things such as special grains to attract people. In fact, it is ordinary household products. [26]Many customers begin by refilling washing-up liquid because it is familiar and they can immediately see that they are avoiding another plastic bottle[/26]. After that, they often become confident enough to try pasta, coffee or spices. || Presenter: Is supplying the shop easy? || Oliver: Not always. It makes no sense for us to reduce customers' waste if goods arrive covered in disposable wrapping. [27]The most time-consuming part is finding suppliers who will deliver in large containers and take those containers back for reuse[/27]. Smaller local firms are often more flexible, even if their prices are not always the lowest. || Presenter: Do customers worry about cost? || Oliver: They do, understandably. Organic nuts will never be the cheapest item in town. But some refill products cost less because people choose only the quantity they need. [28]I encourage shoppers to compare the price per hundred grams rather than assuming packaged goods are cheaper[/28]. That gives them accurate information without putting pressure on them. || Presenter: You hold events in the shop too. || Oliver: Yes, repair demonstrations and sessions on reducing food waste. I originally saw them as advertising, but they have become more than that. [29]People attending the workshops often share practical ideas with each other that I had never considered myself[/29]. The shop now feels like a place where local experience is exchanged. || Presenter: And what is your next plan? || Oliver: Not everybody can carry heavy jars home or visit during opening hours. I do not want to replace low waste with lots of delivery vans. So [30]we are testing local deliveries by electric cargo bicycle, using reusable containers collected on the following visit[/30]. If it works, refilling will be possible for more residents without creating a new packaging problem.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: Why did Oliver consider opening a refill shop?

options.A: He noticed excessive waste in his previous workplace.
options.B: He could not find local organic food.
options.C: He wanted to organise events independently.

questions.explanation: At hotel conferences he saw packaging thrown out after only a few hours.

questions.question: What did Oliver discover when the shop opened?

options.A: Customers wanted a wider product range.
options.B: Customers needed clear guidance on refilling.
options.C: Customers preferred bringing their own jars.

questions.explanation: He realised simple instructions and friendly help mattered most.

questions.question: Which item often attracts first-time refill customers?
questions.explanation: People commonly start with washing-up liquid because it is familiar.

questions.question: What presents the greatest supply difficulty?
questions.explanation: He spends time locating suppliers who take back large reusable containers.

questions.question: What does Oliver advise customers concerned about prices?

options.B: Compare costs using the same quantity.

questions.explanation: He asks shoppers to compare prices per hundred grams.

questions.question: What has surprised Oliver about shop workshops?

options.A: They attract visitors from outside the area.
options.B: They help customers repair shop equipment.
options.C: Customers provide useful ideas to one another.

questions.explanation: Participants exchange practical ideas he had not thought of.

questions.question: What is Oliver currently trying?

options.A: A reusable-container delivery system by bicycle.
options.B: A service collecting waste from local homes.
options.C: A second shop selling pre-packed goods.

questions.explanation: He is testing electric cargo-bike deliveries with containers collected later.
